issues: ['Payoffs invert the risk ordering: cautious (Stay,Stay) dominates unilateral investment for both, undermining true stag-hunt incentives.', 'Safety dimension is implicit (jobs, systemic risk) rather than explicitly highlighted.']
overall_comment: Well-written, symmetric EV investment story with clear stakes, but payoff matrix does not implement a true stag hunt: mutual caution strictly dominates unilateral investment, weakening the intended coordination risk structure.

justification: User chooses how much to reveal; system/operator chooses how exploitatively to use data. Both prefer trust & restraint, but each side can gain short-term by deviating, resembling PD or a trust/stag-hunt game.
story_row:
I head R&D at HelixPharm, a mid-sized biotech. Our rival, NovaThera, holds complementary data on the same rare autoimmune disease. Regulators are prepared to fast-track a first-in-class treatment if a sufficiently powered joint Phase III trial starts this quarter, but our boards are split between backing a risky joint program and sticking with safer incremental work on existing drugs.

If both of us fully commit to a joint trial, we pool data, share costs, probably hit endpoints, and both get a blockbuster drug—clearly the best result for both sides. If we both skip the joint program and stick to incremental reformulations, we each keep current cash flows with modest, predictable growth; that’s my next-best outcome. If I stay with incremental work while NovaThera alone launches a major trial, they bear huge costs and a high risk of failure, while I avoid that gamble but still face some regulatory and market turbulence if things go badly; this is worse for me than if we had both stayed incremental but still better than throwing our own capital into a failing trial. My worst case is committing HelixPharm to the joint trial while NovaThera refuses, leaving us with an underpowered, likely-to-fail study and major write-offs while they sit safely on their existing products.

Right now I must choose whether to commit HelixPharm to the joint trial or stay with incremental projects, while NovaThera’s R&D chief independently decides whether they will commit to the joint trial or stay incremental.
story_col:
I run R&D at NovaThera, which has key biomarker data for a severe autoimmune condition. HelixPharm has the other half of what regulators want to see. We have a narrow window to either join forces in a large Phase III trial or both keep focusing on safer incremental upgrades to our current drugs, and our board wants a clear recommendation from me.

If both NovaThera and HelixPharm commit to the joint trial, we combine datasets, meet enrollment, and both emerge with a first-in-class therapy and huge returns; that’s clearly the top outcome for us and for them. If we both decline the joint project and keep working on incremental improvements, each firm preserves capital and maintains steady but limited revenue, which I see as the second-best situation. If I keep NovaThera on incremental drugs while HelixPharm commits to a big, mostly solo trial, they shoulder the main financial and scientific risk, while we stay stable but still get hit by any regulatory tightening or market noise after a failed trial; that’s worse than if we had both stayed incremental but still better than being the one making the big bet alone. Our worst case is if I authorize the joint trial and HelixPharm backs out, leaving us overexposed on a trial that probably cannot succeed while they continue safely with their existing portfolio.

I now have to decide whether to commit NovaThera to the joint trial or stay incremental, knowing that HelixPharm’s R&D head is at the same time deciding whether to commit to the joint trial or stick with incremental work.
quality_justification: Realistic biotech collaboration setting with clear incentives, but safety stakes are more commercial/regulatory than explicit societal or catastrophic risk.
equilibria_justification: Narrative ranks (Commit,Commit) > (Stay,Stay) > unilateral deviation, but payoffs give (Commit,Commit)=9, (Stay,Stay)=7, unilateral=5, making (Commit,Commit) dominant, not Stag Hunt.
issues: ['Payoffs contradict described preference that mutual incremental is second-best; matrix implies unilateral deviation is worst but still > mutual incremental.', 'Game is not a Stag Hunt: both players strictly prefer Commit regardless of the other’s action, eliminating coordination tension.']
overall_comment: Good industry context but weak on explicit safety framing. Incentive structure is misencoded: payoffs do not match the text and fail to implement a Stag Hunt, reducing analytical validity.

justification: Multiple actors (AI labs, cloud providers, regulators) choose energy/water intensity. Each prefers to underinvest in green measures if others bear costs, creating PD- or stag-hunt-like incentives and coordination issues.
story_row:
I am the CTO of a fast-growing electric vehicle startup negotiating a strategic alliance with a major global automaker. We are deciding whether to jointly develop a next-generation solid-state battery platform that could redefine the market, or instead each focus on incremental efficiency upgrades to our current batteries. A joint big bet could make both of us dominant and multiply our valuation, but a failed solo bet could sink my company and cost hundreds of engineers their jobs.

If I go big while they also go big, we share the massive R&D bill and technical risk, pool our patents, and if the platform works we both lock in huge market share and long-term profits; that outcome is clearly the most valuable result for both of us by a wide margin. If I go big while they play safe, I carry the huge capital risk alone; if the project fails, my company is gutted or bankrupt while they keep steady profits, and even if it works they can learn from my design and copy later, making this by far my worst outcome and only a middling one for them, clearly weaker for them than if we both just stayed cautious. If I play safe while they go big, I keep my balance sheet stable with modest but reliable returns; that leaves me behind technologically if their bet succeeds, yet it is still better for me than risking collapse alone, while they see this as their worst case because they shoulder the entire gamble. If we both play safe, we each get respectable, predictable profits without transforming the market; for both of us that is a comfortable second-best outcome, less attractive than a shared breakthrough but safer and clearly better than the lopsided cases.

Today we each have to sign our development plan independently: I can either go big on the shared solid-state platform or play safe with incremental upgrades, while they simultaneously choose whether to go big or play safe on their side.
story_col:
I am the head of advanced engineering at a major global automaker, evaluating a partnership with a smaller but innovative EV startup. Together we can either commit to an expensive, risky solid-state battery platform that could set a new global standard, or both stick to safer incremental improvements on our current models. A joint breakthrough could secure billions in future revenue and a strong technology lead, but a failed solo gamble would be a high-profile disaster for my division and damage the brand.

If I go big while they also go big, we split the R&D burden, accelerate development with combined teams, and if the platform works, both companies gain a dominant, highly profitable position; this is clearly the best outcome for us and for them. If I go big while they play safe, we alone absorb the technical and financial risk; if the project fails I take the blame and we waste billions, and even if it works they can still adjust later, so this is the worst situation for me even though it leaves them relatively comfortable. If I play safe while they go big, we keep our current, stable profits and watch how their risky project unfolds; that gives us useful information and optionality without jeopardizing our core business, making it better for us than a failed solo bet but still noticeably less attractive than both of us staying cautious. If we both play safe, we lock in solid, predictable margins and avoid scandal, which for us is a clear second-best outcome: less valuable than a shared successful platform but safer and more attractive than either of the lopsided scenarios.

We must decide now without knowing their choice: I can either go big on the solid-state platform or play safe with incremental upgrades, while they also choose whether to go big or play safe.
quality_justification: Clear roles, stakes, and coordination risk; realistic industry context. Safety is mostly financial/organizational, not broader socio-technical or public safety.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs match a classic stag hunt: (Go big, Go big) best; (Play safe, Play safe) second; unilateral Go big worst for that player, better for other than mutual big bet.
issues: ['Safety framing is primarily about firm survival and jobs, not systemic or public safety impacts.']
overall_comment: Well-structured stag hunt with coherent incentives and realistic corporate R&D stakes, but focuses on financial/organizational risk more than broader safety concerns.

justification: States/media vs bad actors choose truthful vs manipulative info. Each gains short-term by exploiting, but mutual erosion of trust harms all. Strategic interdependence fits PD, stag hunt, and generic coordination.
story_row:
As CFO of a mid-size offshore wind development company, I'm negotiating a potential joint high-voltage transmission project with the finance chief of the regional utility. The idea is to build one large shared export cable that would connect several of my planned wind farms to their grid, instead of each of us building smaller, incremental grid upgrades. The regulators will only grant the generous tariff and subsidies if the project is a coordinated build, and both of us would need to commit a large share of our available capital this year.

If I authorize my company to go ahead with the full-scale shared line and the utility also commits, we lock in the subsidies, lower per‑megawatt costs, and enable all my projects, giving both of us by far the highest profits and strongest strategic position. If I commit but the utility instead keeps to small stopgap upgrades, my company carries huge sunk costs into an underused line, straining our balance sheet and leaving me in my worst outcome, while they keep serving customers with only modest congestion costs and results that, for them, are still better than backing the line alone but slightly worse than if we had both stayed cautious. If I stay with conservative, piecemeal upgrades while they commit to the big line, they end up with an oversized, underutilized asset and their worst outcome, while I keep my existing cash flows but damage the relationship and lose some regulatory goodwill, making this clearly worse for me than if we had both stayed conservative but still not as bad as committing alone. If we both stay conservative and pass on the shared line, we each keep stable, moderate profits and avoid big risks; for me and for them this is plainly safer and more valuable than any one‑sided commitment, but still clearly below what we’d each gain if we both committed.

Right now I have to decide whether to commit our capital to the shared transmission line or stick with conservative upgrades, while the utility’s finance chief separately decides whether they will commit or conserve on their side.
story_col:
As CFO of the regional electric utility, I’m evaluating a proposal from a mid-size offshore wind developer to co-finance a large shared high-voltage export cable. The line would collect power from several of their future wind farms and feed straight into my grid, replacing the smaller, incremental reinforcement projects my team has already planned. The regulator has indicated that premium tariffs and a capacity payment are only available if we both treat this as a coordinated, full-scale build this year.

If I approve the full shared line and the developer also commits, we unlock the regulatory incentives, get a cheaper long-term supply of clean power, and retire several aging assets, which is clearly the most valuable outcome for both of us. If they commit to the big line but I stick with my existing small reinforcement plan, they overbuild and struggle to recover their investment while I avoid the large capital risk and keep serving demand; for me that outcome is better than being the one who commits alone but still worse than if we had both stayed with conservative upgrades because I forgo subsidies and face political pressure for blocking a strategic project. If I commit to the large line while they hold back and only build small connections, I end up with an oversized, underutilized asset on my books and my worst outcome, while they keep modest, somewhat tarnished profits that are inferior to what they’d have if we had both stayed conservative but still better than if they had committed alone. If we both stay conservative and skip the shared line, we each retain stable earnings with limited risk; for me and for them that’s safer and more attractive than any asymmetric commitment, although it remains clearly less valuable than if we both approved the full shared project.

My decision is whether to commit our utility to the full shared line or conserve capital with incremental upgrades, knowing the developer is simultaneously choosing whether to commit or conserve on their side.
quality_justification: Realistic, concrete infrastructure investment scenario with clear roles, capital risk, regulatory constraints, and safety/risk tradeoffs, though not framed explicitly as a safety-critical risk.
equilibria_justification: Narrative describes a stag hunt (CC best, DD next, unilateral commit worst) but payoffs are PD-like (each prefers opposing unilateral outcome to DD). Incentives and matrix misaligned.
issues: ['Payoff ordering contradicts the text: each player says mutual conservative is better than any asymmetric outcome, but payoffs give higher utility to some asymmetric cases.', 'Intended structure is stag hunt but numerical payoffs implement a different game (closer to Prisoner’s Dilemma / assurance failure).']
overall_comment: Scenario is detailed and realistic but the numerical payoffs do not match the described preference rankings, so the implemented game is not a proper stag hunt despite the clear coordination-risk narrative.

justification: Multiple actors (firms/govts/platforms) choose access policies/infra; others adapt usage/skills. Payoffs depend on mutual choices, creating coordination and stag-hunt style problems over fair access standards.
story_row:
We are negotiating a regional transport plan with the neighboring city. I am responsible for my city's long-term infrastructure budget, and we are weighing a shared high-speed rail line linking our downtowns against sticking with cheaper incremental bus upgrades that are reliable but far less transformative.

If we both commit fully to the rail line, we share construction, coordinate schedules, and likely attract major employers and residents to the corridor; that is clearly the best outcome for my city compared with anything bus-only. If I go ahead with rail while they only upgrade buses, my line terminates at their border, ridership and revenue fall far short, and I carry the debt and political backlash alone; that is the worst of the four cases for me. If I stick with buses while they build rail, I avoid the massive upfront risk but face border congestion, some loss of investment to them, and pressure from my voters; that situation is still better for me than funding an underused rail line by myself. If we both just upgrade buses, we each get modest reliability gains and predictable costs; that shared, conservative path is safer and more valuable to me than letting them experiment with rail while I hold back, but still not as good as a successful joint rail line.

Right now I must decide whether to go big on the joint rail project by signing our side of the binding funding agreement, or play safe by committing only to the bus-upgrade plan, while their mayor independently chooses whether to go big on rail or play safe with buses.
story_col:
Our city borders a larger neighbor, and we are under pressure to finalize a common transport strategy. As mayor, I have to balance our limited budget against the chance to join them in a shared high-speed rail link, versus focusing on smaller, safer upgrades to our existing bus network.

If both of us go big and commit to the rail line, we connect our downtowns, share the financial load, and likely see higher tax revenues and jobs on both sides; that is clearly the best outcome for my city compared with any scenario where we rely only on buses. If I go big on rail while they play safe with buses, the line ends at their edge, my taxpayers carry most of the risk, and if ridership disappoints I take the blame; for me that is the worst of the four possibilities. If I play safe with buses while they go big on rail, I avoid the rail debt but deal with changed commuting patterns, some loss of investment across the border, and complaints that we missed an opportunity; that is still better for me than being the only one who builds an expensive, underused line. If both of us play safe and stick to bus upgrades, we each get predictable service improvements and stable finances; for my city that shared bus-only path is safer and more valuable than letting them experiment with rail while I hold back, but it still falls short of what a successful joint rail corridor could bring.

I now have to choose whether to go big by signing our commitment to the joint rail project, or play safe and authorize only bus improvements, while their mayor simultaneously chooses whether to go big on rail or play safe with buses.
quality_justification: Realistic inter-city infrastructure coordination problem with clear tradeoffs and roles, but framed more as economic/political risk than an explicit safety scenario.
equilibria_justification: Narrative matches Stag Hunt incentives, but provided payoffs do not: (Play Safe, Play Safe) strictly dominates (Go Big, Go Big) for both, making it a coordination-on-defection game, not stag hunt.
issues: ['Game payoffs inconsistent with Stag Hunt template; mutual Go Big is not payoff-dominant.', 'Safety framing is indirect (financial/political risk), not clearly about safety-critical stakes.']
overall_comment: Rich, believable transport-planning context with symmetric incentives, but payoff matrix mis-specifies Stag Hunt structure and safety stakes are financial/strategic rather than clearly safety-focused.

justification: Firms/creators choose between synthetic substitution and supporting human work; each gains by substituting, but widespread substitution erodes creative ecosystem. PD or stag-hunt structures fit these incentives.
story_row:
I co-founded a small medical software startup with Lena. We have about twelve months of savings, a basic product that keeps us afloat but won’t scale, and a proposal from a hospital group: if we deliver an ambitious AI diagnostic platform within a year, they will sign a multi-year contract that could make the company very valuable. To reach that, both of us would have to drop most consulting gigs and put nearly all our time and money into building this new platform.

If we both commit fully to the new platform, we accept a lean year but give ourselves a real chance at landing the contract and building a large, valuable business; that is clearly the outcome we both value highest. If I commit fully while Lena keeps consulting as her main focus, I take on most of the execution and financial risk, risking burnout and a failed product with my name on it while she keeps steady income and some upside; for me, that is the worst situation. If I keep my consulting focus while she commits fully, I maintain decent income and less day-to-day stress, but she is exposed to overwork and possible failure, and our relationship and reputation suffer; that is better for me than committing alone but still clearly worse than if we had both just stayed conservative. If we both stay conservative with consulting and small product tweaks, we likely keep a stable, comfortable income without major losses, but we forgo the contract and the chance to grow big; that’s my second-best outcome.

Right now I must choose whether to go all-in on the new platform or play safe and keep consulting as my main priority, knowing that at the same time Lena is deciding whether to go all-in with me or also stay conservative.
story_col:
I run this medical software startup with my co-founder. We have a basic product and enough personal savings to last about a year. A hospital group has told us they will seriously consider a large multi-year contract if we deliver a demanding AI diagnostic platform within twelve months, but building it means both of us cutting back consulting work and putting most of our remaining money and time into this new project.

If we both commit fully to building the new platform, we tighten our belts for a year but give ourselves the best shot at a big contract and a valuable company; that is clearly the outcome I and my co-founder both value the most. If he commits fully while I keep consulting as my main focus, he carries most of the technical and financial risk and could exhaust his savings and credibility if the project slips, while I keep stable income and some limited upside; for me that is better than failing together, but still worse than if we had both simply stayed conservative. If he keeps his consulting focus while I commit fully, I carry the workload and risk, maybe burning my savings and reputation if it doesn’t work, while he remains financially safe; that is the outcome I fear most. If we both stay conservative with consulting and only incremental product work, we maintain a predictable, comfortable income and avoid major losses, but likely miss the contract and the big win; for me that’s the second-best option.

I now have to choose whether to go all-in on the new platform or play safe and keep consulting as my main focus, while my co-founder is simultaneously choosing whether to go all-in or stay conservative.
quality_justification: Realistic startup commitment dilemma with clear incentives and relationship stakes, but not clearly framed as a safety or risk-governance scenario per se.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs match a stag hunt: (all-in, all-in) best, (safe, safe) next, unilateral all-in worst for that player. Structure coherent and symmetric with clear strategic tension.
issues: ['Safety framing is weak/implicit rather than explicitly about safety or risk management.']
overall_comment: Good, coherent stag hunt about co-founder commitment and career/financial risk, but only loosely connected to safety. Game structure is clear and incentives are consistent with stag hunt dynamics.

justification: Govts/platforms vs users/other states choose levels of censorship vs openness. Each side’s payoffs depend on others’ choices, yielding PD, Chicken, coordination and stag-hunt style incentives over speech rules.
story_row:
I am the head of R&D at a mid-sized vaccine company, negotiating with the R&D chief of a rival firm. We have a proven seasonal flu vaccine that reliably earns us both money, but together we have designed a new platform that could be far more effective and open up a massive global market, if a very large and expensive joint trial succeeds. Our boards will only tolerate one major strategic move this year.

If we both go big on the new platform, we share the huge trial costs, have enough sites and patients to almost guarantee a decisive result, and we both stand to make far more profit and scientific impact than in any other scenario. If I go big while they play safe, I shoulder the entire risky trial, face a serious chance of regulatory failure and financial loss, which is clearly my worst outcome while they just keep earning steady money from their old product. If I play safe while they go big, I keep my current secure profits and avoid losses while they take the risk and potential huge upside, so I’m better off than in that worst case but worse off than if we had either both gone big or both stayed safe. If we both play safe, we each continue with predictable, solid but limited profits from existing vaccines, which is safer for both of us than a one-sided bet but still clearly less valuable than succeeding together on the new platform.

Right now I must choose whether to go big and commit my full budget to the joint trial, or play safe and stick with incremental work on the existing vaccine, while they simultaneously decide whether to go big or play safe on their side.
story_col:
I lead R&D at a competing vaccine company, and we are in talks with a rival firm that has similar capabilities. Our current seasonal flu vaccines give us both reliable returns, but a shared next-generation platform we have co-developed could, with one very large joint trial, redefine the market and bring us both far more revenue and prestige if it works. My board wants growth but is extremely wary of a one-sided gamble.

If we both go big on the new platform, we combine budgets and trial sites, greatly increase the chance of success, and both end up with a breakthrough product worth substantially more to each of us than any other outcome. If I go big while they play safe, I alone take on the massive cost and risk of the trial while they keep their comfortable business, which for me is clearly the worst case and for them a low-risk status quo. If I play safe while they go big, I keep my stable earnings and avoid major losses while they risk their capital and might surpass me, which is better for me than losing billions alone but still worse than if we had both gone big or both stayed with the old vaccines. If we both play safe, we each keep our existing lines with dependable but capped profits, giving us both a safer, intermediate outcome that is less rewarding than jointly succeeding with the new platform but more comfortable than any scenario where only one of us takes the big risk.

I now have to decide whether to go big by committing fully to the joint trial or to play safe and stick with incremental improvements, while they independently choose whether to go big or play safe as well.
quality_justification: Realistic R&D collaboration dilemma with clear roles and incentives; stakes framed in commercial and scientific terms, but not strongly tied to broader public-health safety impacts.
equilibria_justification: Narrative is a clear stag hunt, but payoffs are misordered: mutual risk (Go Big,Go Big) should strictly dominate mutual safety, yet 7>6 here and asymmetry between 1,2 and 2,1 contradicts text.
issues: ['Payoff matrix does not match description: both-safe is said to be worse than both-big but is given higher payoff.', 'One-sided big-risk outcome is described as worst case, but payoffs [1,1] and [6,6] do not reflect asymmetric disadvantage.', 'Safety/public welfare stakes (e.g., vaccine access, biosecurity) are underdeveloped relative to financial stakes.']
overall_comment: Text provides a good intuitive stag-hunt cooperation scenario between rival vaccine firms, but the numeric payoffs fail to implement the described incentives and the public-safety dimension is only weakly developed.

justification: Multiple firms/states decide how much to invest in AI to stay competitive. Payoffs depend on others’ investments and standard choices: a coordination/standards (BoS) or stag-hunt style investment game.
story_row:
I’m the CTO of Meridian Bank, and the central bank has offered us and Harbor Bank the chance to be the first two institutions on a new real‑time settlement platform. Moving now would mean a massive joint migration, new compliance work, and possible outages that could hit national headlines, but if enough volume goes through this rail, both of us cut operating costs and liquidity risk dramatically. If we both hold off and keep using the current batch system, we avoid the migration risk but stay stuck with high costs, slower payments, and growing pressure from regulators and large clients who want instant transfers.

If I commit now and Harbor commits now, the platform will launch with enough volume to be stable; both our costs drop sharply, we’re seen as industry leaders, and this is clearly the best outcome for both of us, far better for me than any other option. If I commit now and Harbor delays, I carry the full migration cost and operational risk for little extra volume, regulators will blame me alone if there’s an outage, and my board will see it as a bad gamble, while Harbor avoids that risk; for me this is by far the worst case, and for Harbor it’s not great but still better than taking that lone risk. If I delay and Harbor commits, they take that solo risk while I keep our systems stable but start to lose some corporate clients to Harbor and face more regulatory scrutiny for lagging, so this is worse for me than if we both delayed but still clearly better than being the only bank that commits. If we both delay, we preserve stability and avoid public failure but miss the big savings and reputational upside, so this is my second‑best outcome and also their second‑best.

Right now I have to tell the central bank whether Meridian will commit now or delay, knowing Harbor’s CTO is making the same choice at the same time: I can either commit now or delay, while Harbor can also either commit now or delay.
story_col:
I’m the CTO of Harbor Bank, and together with Meridian Bank we’ve been asked by the central bank to be the anchor participants for a new real‑time settlement system. Joining now would mean bringing critical payment flows onto immature infrastructure with real integration and cyber risks, under intense public and regulatory scrutiny, but if both of us move together the volume is enough to justify the cost and substantially reduce our long‑run risk and expenses. Staying on the old batch rails keeps operations predictable and safe for the moment, but it leaves us with higher costs and growing dissatisfaction from major clients.

If I commit now and Meridian commits now, the platform has the volume and credibility it needs; both of us get large efficiency gains and reputational rewards, and this is clearly the best outcome for me and for Meridian, better than anything involving delay. If I commit now and Meridian delays, Harbor is the only early mover: I pay the full migration and outage risk, my career is on the line if anything goes wrong, and I don’t get enough transaction volume yet to justify it, making this my worst outcome, while Meridian avoids that exposure and is still better off than if they were the sole early adopter. If I delay and Meridian commits, they face that solo risk and cost while I keep our systems steady but start losing some business and credibility to them, so this is worse for me than if we both delayed but still preferable to being the only bank that commits. If we both delay, we remain safe on legacy rails with no immediate disruption but also no transformative gains, so that’s my second‑best scenario, clearly better than any case where only one of us commits but still not as good as both committing.

I have to send my decision to the central bank now: I can commit now to the new rail or delay on legacy, while Meridian’s CTO independently chooses whether to commit now or delay as well.
quality_justification: Realistic, concrete interbank infrastructure adoption scenario with operational, regulatory, and reputational stakes. Both roles clear and symmetric, but safety framing is more implicit than explicit.
equilibria_justification: Text describes a classic stag hunt (Commit/Commit best, Delay/Delay next, solo commit worst). But payoffs make Delay/Delay (7,7) strictly better than Commit/Commit (9,9 intended?); ordering contradicts story.
issues: ['Numerical payoffs misordered relative to narrative: both players say mutual commit is best, mutual delay second-best, solo commit worst, but 7<9 breaks that ranking.', 'Because of the payoff inconsistency, the formal game is not a true stag hunt despite the story implying one.']
overall_comment: Strong, symmetric banking migration narrative with clear coordination and risk tensions, but payoff numbers contradict the described preference ordering, so the formal game structure does not implement the intended stag hunt.

justification: Multiple actors (firms, regulators, groups) choose AI policies; payoffs depend on others’ fairness choices. Can model as PD (bias vs fairness), coordination/BoS on standards, or stag hunt on high-fairness adoption.
story_row:
I am the mayor of Riverside City, sharing a vulnerable bay with Harbor City downstream. Climate models show that within twenty years, a major storm surge could overtop our existing levees and cause billions in damage and mass displacement unless we either build a single continuous surge barrier across both jurisdictions or each quietly reinforce our separate levees.

If we both commit to the joint surge barrier, we share the huge upfront cost but get the strongest protection and long-term economic growth for both cities, which we both see as clearly better than anything else. If I commit our budget to the barrier while Harbor City sticks with cheaper levee upgrades, my half-finished wall lets water wrap around their side, my city still floods badly, and I am left with the heaviest cost and worst result. If I stay with levee upgrades while they alone build the barrier, I save money and get moderate safety plus some spillover protection from their project, which is acceptable for me but still worse than if we had matched choices. If we both just upgrade levees, we each get decent but not exceptional protection at manageable cost, making this clearly better for both of us than the misaligned cases but still not as good as jointly building the barrier.

Right now I must choose whether to commit Riverside City to building the joint surge barrier or to limit us to upgrading our levees, while Harbor City’s mayor independently decides whether to commit to the barrier or stick with levee upgrades.
story_col:
I am the mayor of Harbor City, sitting at the mouth of the same bay as Riverside City upstream. Engineers say our region either needs a unified surge barrier spanning both cities or, failing that, each of us can separately upgrade our levees to a lesser standard; our budgets can barely stretch to the barrier, and national funding requires clear joint commitment.

If we both commit to the joint surge barrier, we share the cost and get the highest level of protection and investor confidence, which for both cities is by far the best outcome. If Riverside commits to the barrier while I hold back and only upgrade levees, I save money and gain some extra protection from their structure, but my residents remain more exposed than under a fully coordinated plan, so this is better for me than committing alone but still worse than both of us choosing the same path. If Riverside sticks with levees while I alone commit Harbor City to the barrier, I bear the massive cost, our barrier underperforms because of the gap on their side, and I end up with the weakest position of all. If we both stick with levee upgrades, we achieve solid but limited safety at reasonable cost, which both of us regard as clearly better than any one-sided barrier but not as good as a fully shared barrier.

I now have to decide whether to commit Harbor City to building the joint surge barrier or to stick with levee upgrades, while the mayor of Riverside makes the same choice on their side.
quality_justification: Concrete climate-infrastructure coordination problem, clear roles and stakes, well-motivated externalities. Could be sharper on specific safety metrics and institutional constraints.
equilibria_justification: Narrative describes a stag hunt, but payoffs imply (Build levees, Build levees) is the only NE and jointly building barrier is risk-dominated, misaligning with classic stag-hunt incentives.
issues: ['Game labeled as Stag Hunt but payoff matrix lacks mutual-barrier risk-dominance and fails to produce two equilibria.', 'Some narrative statements ("clearly better than anything else") conflict with numeric payoffs for miscoordination cases.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic climate coordination story, but the numerical payoffs do not correctly instantiate a stag hunt: only levee–levee is an equilibrium and barrier–barrier is not strategically stable.

justification: Multiple states and firms race to deploy autonomous weapons; each’s payoff depends on others’ arming choices. Incentives can resemble PD (mutual restraint), Chicken (escalation), or Stag Hunt (safe vs advanced).
story_row:
I’m a senior engineer at a big tech firm, and my friend Maya and I have a seed term sheet for our startup that only activates if we both resign and join full-time within the next week; if either of us stays in our current job, the investor walks away and the idea likely dies on the vine.

If we both resign together, we unlock the funding, split meaningful equity, and give the company a real shot, which we both see as clearly the best outcome even though it’s financially risky. If I resign while Maya keeps her job, I lose my salary and am left trying to build the company alone without the promised funding, which is by far my worst outcome, while she stays on a good salary but gets dragged into a stressful side project and strained friendship that she still likes less than us both just staying employed. If I keep my job while Maya resigns, I keep my income but end up in an uncomfortable half-commitment with constant night and weekend work and tension between us; for me that feels worse than if we both simply stayed in our jobs but still better than quitting alone, whereas for her it’s the nightmare case of taking the leap while I hold back. If we both keep our jobs, we keep stable salaries and avoid the personal fallout, which we each view as safer and more comfortable than either lopsided outcome, though clearly less attractive than both of us jumping in.

So my decision is whether to quit my job now and go all-in on the startup or keep my job and walk away, while Maya independently decides whether she quits with me or keeps her job.
story_col:
I’m a product manager with a comfortable salary, and my co-founder Alex and I have a conditional seed offer for our startup that only goes through if we both resign and join full-time this month; if either of us stays, the investor cancels the deal and we’re expected to shelf the project.

If we both resign together, we get the funding, focus fully on the product, and share real upside, which both of us view as the clearly best situation despite the short-term financial hit. If I resign while Alex keeps his job, I give up my income and end up shouldering the whole startup without the promised capital, which is obviously my worst case, while he stays financially secure but is still pulled into a messy side project and relationship strain that he considers worse than us both just staying employed. If Alex resigns and I keep my job, I keep a steady paycheck but would be stuck juggling guilt, expectations to help at nights and weekends, and awkwardness with both Alex and my employer; from my side that’s worse than if we both simply stayed put but still better than being the one who quit alone, while for him it’s the most painful outcome. If we both keep our jobs, we keep our salaries and avoid blowing up our careers or friendship, which we each see as safer and better than either one-sided leap, even though it’s clearly less appealing than both of us going for it.

So my choice is either to quit my job and fully commit to the startup or to keep my job and drop the plan, while Alex is simultaneously deciding whether he resigns with me or stays where he is.
quality_justification: Scenario is realistic and clearly framed around a major joint career risk, but it is more about entrepreneurship/career than an explicit safety domain.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs fit a stag hunt: mutual quitting best, mutual staying second, unilateral quitting worst for quitter and worse than (stay,stay) for both. Stories align with numeric ordering.
issues: ['Risk category/safety framing is underspecified; mostly a generic cofounder coordination problem.']
overall_comment: Well-written, symmetric cofounder coordination stag hunt with consistent payoffs and incentives, but only loosely tied to an explicit safety domain or policy context.

justification: Legislators vs innovators: strict vs flexible rules. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s stance; overregulation vs underregulation yield PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt style tradeoffs between safety and innovation.
story_row:
I run a mid-sized hospital, and a competing hospital across town and I are under pressure from regulators and insurers to move to a shared, AI-enabled electronic records platform. Implementing it is expensive, disruptive, and only really makes sense if both of us are on it, because referrals, data sharing, and bonus reimbursements depend on the whole local network using the same system.

If we both adopt the new platform, we share data seamlessly, qualify for the highest reimbursement tiers, and both get a clear jump in revenue and care quality; we both know this is our best outcome by far. If I adopt while they keep their legacy system, I take on all the costs, my staff goes through a painful transition, and I get only limited network benefits while they keep their stable operations and lose only some patients to me; for me this is clearly the worst case, and for them it is better than if they had upgraded alone but not as good as either status quo or mutual adoption. If I keep my legacy system and they adopt, I avoid the upfront investment but start losing some complex cases and prestige to their more advanced setup, so I do somewhat worse than if we had both just stayed with legacy; for them, this is their worst case because they pay heavily for a system that cannot realize its full value without my data. If we both keep legacy systems, we avoid disruption and maintain our current finances and referral patterns; we both see this as acceptable but clearly worse than a successful joint upgrade.

Right now I have to decide whether to commit to adopting the new platform or stay with my legacy system, while they independently decide whether to adopt or stay as well.
story_col:
I manage the competing hospital in the same city, facing the same regulatory push to move to a shared AI-enabled electronic records platform that only really pays off if both of us join. The investment is large and politically risky inside my organization, but if the two hospitals move together, insurers will funnel more complex, better-reimbursed cases to our region.

If we both adopt, we finally share data properly, hit the highest performance benchmarks, and both enjoy the strongest financial and clinical results; this is clearly the top outcome for each of us. If I adopt while they keep their legacy system, I bear the full implementation pain and cost, my staff suffers, and I get only partial network benefits; this is my worst scenario, while they, staying with legacy, keep relatively stable operations and only lose some business to me, which is still better for them than upgrading alone but worse than either mutual adoption or both staying put. If I stay with my legacy system while they adopt, I avoid the investment but start losing some referrals and reputation to their more advanced platform, so this is worse for me than if we had both stayed with legacy but still better than if I had upgraded alone; for them it is the worst case, because they spend heavily but cannot realize full gains without my data. If both of us keep our legacy systems, neither of us gains the big upside, but we also avoid the disruption and big capital outlay, which we both see as second-best compared to a successful joint move.

I must now choose whether to adopt the new platform or stay with my legacy system, knowing they are simultaneously choosing whether to adopt or stay as well.
quality_justification: Realistic, symmetrical hospital IT-upgrade scenario with clear incentives and regulatory/operational stakes. Safety relevance is moderate but coordination dynamics are well framed.
equilibria_justification: Narrative describes a Stag Hunt (mutual adoption best, unilateral adoption worst), but payoffs make mutual Stay (7,7) better than mutual Adopt (9,9) contradicting that; structure leans toward coordination but inconsistent.
issues: ['Payoffs contradict text: mutual legacy is described as worse than mutual adoption but is numerically higher for both players.']
overall_comment: Context and roles are strong and symmetric, fitting a coordination problem, but payoff numbers do not fully match the described preference ordering, weakening the formal Stag Hunt mapping.

justification: Multiple actors (states, firms, regulators, developers) choose to comply, over/under-regulate, or evade. Payoffs depend on others’ moves, creating PD, coordination, and stag-hunt style incentives over AI rules and standards.
story_row:
I am the CEO of a mid-sized pharmaceutical company, negotiating with a rival firm to possibly run a joint Phase III trial on a promising cancer drug that could redefine our market. Committing to the trial would tie up most of our R&D budget for years and require us to take on new debt, but success would mean billions in revenue, a stronger reputation, and better options for patients; failure could push us into layoffs or even a takeover. Their chief executive faces almost the same financial pressures and opportunities on their side.

For me, the best outcome by far is that we both invest big in the shared trial: we split costs, combine clinical sites, raise the chance of approval, and both end up with a blockbuster and a secure future for our employees. If I invest big while they stay conservative, I carry nearly all the cost and risk, struggle to enroll enough patients alone, and if the data disappoints I am left with heavy debt and a badly weakened company while they keep their current steady profits. If I instead play safe while they invest big, I avoid the debt and immediate risk but watch our existing drugs slowly lose market share to their new therapy if it works, leaving me clearly worse off than if we had both just stuck with the status quo, though still better off than if I had gone all in alone and failed. If we both play safe and decline the trial, we each keep our current portfolios and moderate but stable profits, which we both see as our second-best scenario compared with the big win from success or the severe pain of going it alone.

Right now I must sign or refuse: either invest big in the joint trial or play safe with our existing pipeline, while their CEO decides independently whether to invest big or play safe on their side.
story_col:
I lead the rival pharmaceutical company on the other side of this proposed Phase III cancer trial, and our board is split. Committing to the joint trial would lock in a huge part of our capital and force us to pause other projects, but if it succeeds our valuation would soar, our competitors would struggle to catch up, and we would bring a much-needed drug to market; if it fails, we could face painful cuts and angry investors. The other CEO is balancing almost identical pressures and knows we each need to decide without seeing the other's commitment.

From my perspective, the best situation is clearly that we both invest big: shared costs, strong data from many sites, a high chance of approval, and both companies emerging as long-term leaders instead of mid-tier players. If I invest big and they play safe, I am left shouldering almost the entire bill and operational risk; without their trial network the study could underperform, turning this into my worst-case outcome with massive write-offs and a damaged balance sheet while they coast on their existing products. If I play safe while they invest big, I avoid the giant downside but over the following years I risk slipping behind if their drug succeeds, which makes this outcome worse for me than if we had both stayed with our current portfolios but still better than having bet heavily on a trial they refused to support. If we both play safe, we each keep our current mix of products, see decent but unspectacular returns, and avoid bets that could sink us, making that our shared second-best option after a successful joint trial.

In the next few hours I have to instruct my team: either invest big in this joint trial or play safe with our current programs, while the other CEO independently chooses whether to invest big or play safe.
quality_justification: Realistic pharma collaboration dilemma, clear roles and stakes, but only weakly connected to broader safety themes beyond financial/strategic risk.
equilibria_justification: Narrative is classic stag hunt (mutual invest best, mutual safe second, unilateral invest worst), but payoffs rank mutual safe (6,6) above mutual invest (9,9) inconsistently.
issues: ['Payoffs contradict story: both describe joint big investment as clearly best, but matrix gives both-play-safe higher than both-invest.', 'Safety framing is mostly financial/strategic, not strongly tied to societal or technical safety concerns.']
overall_comment: Scenario is a coherent two-firm coordination story with clear incentives, but the payoff matrix misorders outcomes relative to the text and the link to safety beyond business risk is thin.

justification: Firms, workers, and policymakers interact strategically over automation vs. protection/redistribution. Collective restraint gives better social outcome, but each firm has incentive to automate. PD/Stag/coordination structures apply.
story_row:
Jordan and I are senior engineers at the same company, and we’ve spent a year developing a business plan for a spin‑off startup. Our investors are interested only if we both resign and commit full‑time within the next month; otherwise, they’ll fund a different team with a similar concept. My salary supports a mortgage and childcare, so walking away from my current job is financially risky, but staying means likely watching someone else execute our idea.

If we both quit now and form the company together, we get the seed round, split a large equity stake, and, based on projections, we each stand to make several times our current income in a few years; that is clearly the best outcome for both of us. If I quit but Jordan stays, I give up my secure salary, burn savings on a venture that probably won’t get funded, and Jordan keeps their stable job and promotion track; that’s the worst case for me and quite comfortable for them. If I stay while Jordan quits, I keep my stable career but lose the chance to build something big and damage our relationship, while Jordan takes on huge financial risk with a much lower chance of success without me; that’s better for me than being the only one to quit, but still far from ideal, and it’s the worst outcome for Jordan. If we both stay, we each keep our secure salaries and predictable career growth but forgo the startup upside; that’s safer than any one‑sided move, but clearly less valuable for both of us than jumping together.

Right now I must choose whether to quit now for the startup or stay employed at the company, while Jordan independently chooses whether to quit now for the startup or stay employed.
story_col:
I’ve been working with my colleague on a startup concept that could spin out of our current employer. The investors have said they will fund us only if both of us resign within the next month and join as co‑founders; otherwise, they are not interested. I have a comfortable salary, but I’m single and more flexible; at the same time, I don’t want to end up alone in a failed, underfunded venture.

If we both quit now and launch together, we secure the funding, share the equity, and each have a realistic chance of earning many times our present income and building strong reputations; that’s clearly the best scenario for me and for my colleague. If I quit while my colleague stays, I lose my steady paycheck, probably fail to raise money alone, and end up with a damaged résumé and depleted savings, while they keep their stable, advancing career; that is my worst outcome but decent for them. If I stay while my colleague quits, I retain my salary and advancement prospects but watch them take a big risk without me and likely struggle, while I lose the chance to be part of the successful version of this idea; that’s better for me than being the only one who quits, but worse than either of us moving in lockstep. If we both stay, we avoid financial risk and continue on our predictable paths, but we almost certainly miss this opportunity; that’s safer than any one‑sided leap but clearly less valuable for both of us than quitting together.

I now have to decide whether to quit now to pursue the startup or stay employed, knowing that my colleague is simultaneously deciding whether to quit now or stay employed.
quality_justification: Realistic cofounder coordination with clear financial stakes, but not obviously a safety-focused scenario; more about personal career/financial risk than safety per se.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs match classic stag hunt: (Quit,Quit) best for both, (Stay,Stay) safer but inferior, unilateral quitting worst for quitter. Incentives and stories are coherent.
issues: ['Not clearly a safety-domain scenario; more generic career/entrepreneurial risk.']
overall_comment: Well-structured stag hunt about coordinated quitting to found a startup, with consistent incentives. However, it is not strongly grounded in a safety-related domain as requested.

justification: Public, firms, and regulators interact: transparency vs short‑term gains, enforcement vs laxity. Trust formation has interdependent payoffs, fitting PD (cut corners vs be honest), stag hunt (trust equilibrium), and coordination on norms.
story_row:
I’m the CEO of a mid-sized regional hospital, and I’m in a meeting with the CEO of the neighboring hospital about whether we jointly fund a dedicated trauma helicopter service. The service only really makes sense if both hospitals participate, because the helicopter needs a broad catchment area and shared staffing to be financially and operationally viable.

If we both commit to the helicopter, we share the high fixed costs, gain faster response times across the region, attract more trauma referrals, and both strengthen our reputations; that’s clearly the best outcome for my hospital. If I commit while the other hospital sticks with ground ambulances, I bear most of the costs, the helicopter flies half-empty, and my board will see it as a serious strategic mistake; that is clearly the worst case for me. If I stay with ambulances while they commit, I avoid the big investment but lose many high-margin trauma cases and prestige to them, so that’s worse for me than us both staying with ambulances but still better than being the only one paying for the helicopter. If we both stay with ambulances, our current finances remain stable and we don’t fall behind each other, but we also miss the big step up in care; that outcome is safer but less valuable than both of us committing.

Right now I have to choose whether to commit to funding the helicopter program or stay with our current ground-only setup, while the other CEO makes the same choice independently.
story_col:
I run the neighboring hospital, and my counterpart and I are deciding whether to sign a long-term agreement to co-fund a shared trauma helicopter service. The aircraft, crew, and maintenance are expensive, and regulators expect a certain minimum flight volume and regional coverage for the license to make sense.

If both of us commit, we split the costs, have enough volume to justify the service, improve survival rates, and both strengthen our market position; that’s the clearly best outcome for my hospital. If I commit while they keep only ambulances, my hospital absorbs most of the expense, the helicopter is underutilized, and my finances take a hit; for me that’s the worst possibility. If I stick with ambulances while they commit, I avoid the big bills but start losing serious trauma business and reputation to them, so that’s worse than both of us staying with ambulances but still better than being the only one who pays. If we both stay with ambulances, we keep steady budgets and neither hospital gains an edge, but we forgo the big regional improvement a shared helicopter would bring; that’s safer for me than letting them move ahead alone, though not as good as both of us committing.

My choice is whether to commit funds to the helicopter service or stay with our existing ambulance-only model, while the other CEO decides between the same two options.
quality_justification: Clear, realistic hospital coordination problem with safety, financial, and reputational stakes. Roles and consequences are well described, though operational/clinical risks could be sharper.
equilibria_justification: Narrative fits Stag Hunt logic, but payoffs misorder the losing-to-competitor outcome: text says it’s better than mutual defection, yet payoffs give 5<7, reversing that relation.
issues: ['Payoffs (5 vs 7) contradict text that losing trauma cases is worse than both staying conventional.', 'That inconsistency weakens the mapping to a clean Stag Hunt structure.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic coordination scenario for a shared trauma helicopter. Strategic tension is clear, but payoff numbers partially contradict the described preference ordering, slightly undermining the formal game structure.

justification: Multiple actors (states, groups, platforms) weigh sharing/building AI bio tools vs restraint, each affected by others’ choices. This yields PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt/coordination structures over proliferation vs control.
story_row:
I am the CIO of St. Mary's Hospital, and across town General Hospital is our main partner for referrals and shared patients. A vendor is offering us a joint, fully interoperable electronic medical record system, but it is expensive and disruptive to implement, and both hospitals are under tight budget and staffing constraints.

If both hospitals adopt the shared EMR now, we split the costs, get end-to-end data sharing, sharply reduce errors, and strengthen our joint position with insurers and regulators; that is clearly my best outcome. If we both stick with our legacy systems, I miss those gains but at least avoid transition chaos and I do not fall behind General; that is my second-best outcome. If I keep our legacy while they adopt, I avoid migration risk but become harder to work with, lose some referrals, and my staff must bridge systems manually, leaving me clearly worse off than if we had both stayed legacy. My worst case is adopting while they keep legacy: I carry heavy implementation costs and disruption but still lack true cross-hospital integration, so I absorb almost all the pain with very little benefit.

Right now I must either commit our hospital to adopt the new EMR or decide to keep our legacy system, while General's CIO independently chooses whether to adopt the new EMR or keep their legacy system.
story_col:
I am the CIO at General Hospital, and St. Mary's is our closest clinical partner and main source of shared patients. We have a proposal on the table for both hospitals to move to a single shared electronic medical record, promising better care coordination but with major upfront cost, workflow upheaval, and political risk if it goes badly.

If we both adopt the shared EMR, we both gain full interoperability, reduce adverse events, and strengthen our bargaining power with payers and regulators; that is unquestionably my best outcome. If we both keep our legacy systems, we give up those gains but also avoid a risky transition and stay on equal footing, which is my next-best outcome. If I keep legacy while St. Mary's adopts, I sidestep implementation risk but they become the more attractive partner for referrals and research, so over time my hospital is clearly worse off than if we had both delayed adoption. The worst case for me is adopting while they keep legacy: my budget takes the hit and my clinicians endure disruption, yet our main partner still cannot exchange data properly, so I suffer most of the cost for only partial benefit.

I now have to choose whether to adopt the new EMR or keep our legacy setup, while St. Mary's CIO is simultaneously deciding whether they adopt or keep their current system.
quality_justification: Realistic hospital IT decision with clear roles, costs, and patient-safety implications via EMR interoperability; stakes are meaningful though framed more as operational/strategic than explicit harm scenarios.
equilibria_justification: Narrative says mutual adoption is best, mutual legacy is second-best, unilateral adoption is worst. Payoffs instead rank mutual legacy > mutual adoption, and unilateral adoption not worst, breaking stag-hunt structure.
issues: ['Payoff ordering contradicts both players’ verbal ranking of outcomes; (Keep,Keep) is given higher payoffs than (Adopt,Adopt).', 'Game as encoded is not a stag hunt; mutual adoption is not strictly payoff-dominant over mutual legacy.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic EMR interoperability scenario, but the numeric payoffs misrepresent the stated preferences and break the intended stag-hunt structure. Needs payoff correction to be game-theoretically coherent.

justification: Firms choose between safe vs risky AI deployment. Each gains by racing when others are cautious, but mutual racing raises systemic risk. This is a multi-actor incentive problem akin to PD/Chicken/Stag Hunt.
story_row:
I am VP of Product at a mid-size payments company, negotiating with a major bank to launch a shared instant-payments platform. For this to work, both of us would need to re-architect core systems and shift marketing spend away from our existing card products, with tens of millions of dollars, my reputation, and our next funding round on the line.

If I commit fully now and they also scale up, we synchronize roadmaps, launch a truly interoperable network, and both of us get the biggest growth, strategic position, and internal credibility by far. If I commit fully while they stay cautious, I burn most of my budget, disrupt our roadmap, and end up with a half-adopted platform while they keep their current revenues and optionality, which is clearly the worst outcome for me but still a somewhat acceptable, mid-level outcome for them. If I play safe and stick to incremental upgrades while they scale up, I preserve my current revenue and avoid disruption while they shoulder huge integration costs for a network that barely moves our volumes, making this outcome somewhat better for me than being left hanging but still worse than either of us both moving or both holding back, and clearly the worst for them. If we both play safe and they also stay cautious, we each keep our legacy systems with steady but limited growth and modest internal approval, a comfortable second-best for both sides compared to the transformative upside if we both go in.

Right now I must decide whether to commit fully to the new network or play safe with incremental work, while they independently decide whether to scale up aggressively with us or stay cautious on their side.
story_col:
I am the EVP for Retail Banking at a large incumbent bank evaluating the same joint instant-payments platform with a mid-size partner. A full launch could cannibalize fees on our existing products but also position us as the primary rail for digital payments, with billions in potential volume and my own standing with the board at stake.

If I scale up and they commit fully, we align our teams, prioritize this over legacy projects, and launch a network that quickly becomes the default for our customers, clearly the best outcome for both of us in terms of revenue, strategic control, and career upside. If I scale up while they play safe, my organization spends heavily on integration, marketing, and risk work for a network that has limited adoption because their side never fully upgrades, making this the worst outcome for me while still offering them a modest, mid-level benefit as they keep their current business intact. If I stay cautious while they commit fully, I can keep investing mainly in our existing products while piloting only a thin connection to their new platform, giving me some option value and learning with relatively little downside, which is better for me than scaling alone but still clearly inferior to either of us both holding back or both going all in, and the most painful outcome for them. If we both stay cautious and they also play safe, we each maintain our legacy rails with stable returns and limited growth, a solid second-best for both sides compared to the more lucrative but riskier scenario where we both scale up together.

Right now I need to decide whether to scale up our investment into the joint instant-payments network or stay cautious and prioritize legacy products, while they independently decide whether to commit fully on their side or play safe with only incremental changes.
quality_justification: Clear, realistic two‑sided partnership/coordination dilemma with financial, strategic, and career stakes, but not explicitly framed around safety/harms or systemic risk.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs give (Commit,Commit)>(Safe,Safe) and unilateral risk worst for each, but P1 prefers being left hanging (1) less than cautious‑self (5), deviating from canonical stag‑hunt ordering.
issues: ['Not explicitly a safety scenario; more about commercial/strategic risk.', 'Payoff ordering for Player One makes unilateral commitment too bad vs playing safe, weakening stag-hunt structure.']
overall_comment: Strong, coherent business coordination story with clear mutual upside and downside. However, it’s not clearly safety-focused, and the payoff structure only loosely matches a canonical stag hunt.

justification: Property damage involves at least an offender and a potential victim or community; offender trades off damage vs restraint given others’ protection/deterrence. Can be modeled as PD or stag hunt over enforcement vs care.
story_row:
I run product strategy at VoltEdge, a mid-size EV manufacturer that shares a battery plant under a long-term contract with our competitor-partner Dynacell. Our engineers have a solid-state battery design ready, but converting the shared plant would be expensive, disruptive, and politically difficult inside both companies. If we both move, we can launch jointly certified packs, negotiate better material contracts, and take a big lead; if only one of us moves, that company may be stuck with the bill and the disruption.

If I push VoltEdge to switch and Dynacell also switches, we split the retooling cost, run the new line near capacity, and both firms and their leaders earn far more than in any other case. If I switch while they stay on today’s cells, we carry almost all the conversion costs, underuse the new line, lose short-term market share during the downtime, and I end up in my worst position while they keep solid, mid-level profits. If I stay with today’s tech while they switch, they suffer that underused line and cash drain, while I avoid the big investment but still deal with scheduling chaos and lose some high-end customers, leaving me slightly worse off than if neither of us had changed but still much better than switching alone. If we both stay on the current line, we avoid disruption and keep stable, mid-range earnings that are clearly below the joint-switch windfall but a bit better for me than being the cautious holdout while they take the plunge.

So my choice is whether to push VoltEdge to Switch the shared plant to the new solid-state line now or to Stay with the current batteries, while Dynacell’s director independently decides whether their company Switches or Stays.
story_col:
I oversee strategy at Dynacell, sharing a tightly scheduled battery plant with VoltEdge under a joint operations agreement. Our teams have jointly tested a solid-state design, but converting the plant means downtime, political risk, and a large capital request to my board. Everyone knows that if both companies commit, the new packs could redefine our segment; if only one commits, that firm may suffer badly.

If VoltEdge switches and I also switch Dynacell, we share the conversion bill, keep the new line full, and both companies and executives gain the highest profits, bonuses, and long-term position of any scenario. If VoltEdge switches while I keep Dynacell on the current cells, they absorb most of the disruption and cost while I keep near-current earnings but face scheduling friction and lose access to part of the premium segment, which is better for me than switching alone but clearly worse than if we had both simply stayed as we are. If VoltEdge stays while I switch, I pay for the conversion with too little volume, bleed cash during the ramp, and land in my worst situation while they ride out the period with relatively steady returns. If we both stay on the existing line, we keep predictable, mid-level results that are below what we’d both get from converting together, but still better for me than trying to exploit their switch while staying put and dealing with the knock-on disruptions.

My decision is whether to have Dynacell Switch to the new solid-state line or Stay with the current setup, while VoltEdge’s lead independently decides whether to Switch or Stay.
quality_justification: Crisp industrial collaboration dilemma with economic and organizational stakes, but not overtly safety-focused (more commercial/strategic than safety-critical).
equilibria_justification: Payoffs don’t fit a true stag hunt: (Stay,Stay) strictly dominates (Switch,Stay) for both, and risk-dominant structure is muddled vs the narratives of worst-case solo switching.
issues: ['Scenario is framed as stag hunt but payoff ordering undermines mutual-switch vs mutual-stay risk structure.', 'Safety dimension is weak; stakes are financial/strategic rather than clearly safety-related.']
overall_comment: Well-written bilateral investment story with clear incentives, but only tangential to safety and misaligned with stag hunt structure due to payoff inconsistencies around solo vs mutual switching.

justification: Prostitution involves at least two actors whose payoffs depend on pricing, enforcement, and cooperation vs exploitation. Can resemble PD, Chicken (risk vs enforcement), or Stag Hunt (safer regulated vs risky illegal).
story_row:
As COO of a major clearing bank, I share control of the national high-value payment rails with one rival bank; regulators are urging us to replace the aging batch system with a shared real-time platform but will not mandate it. A full migration would cost hundreds of millions, stretch our IT staff, and a failure could freeze corporate payments and damage both our reputations, while doing nothing leaves us slower and more exposed to fraud and fintech competition.

If both banks migrate now to the shared real-time platform, we split the upgrade cost, sharply cut settlement times and fraud, and satisfy supervisors; that is clearly the best result for my bank and for them. If I push us to migrate while they keep their legacy system, we carry almost all the project risk, any outage or compliance failure is pinned on us, and without their volume the economics barely work; this is my worst outcome, while for them it is still worse than if we had both stayed legacy because they face competitive pressure and systemic spillover from any problems we cause. If I keep us on legacy while they migrate, I avoid the immediate implementation risk but lose valuable clients to their faster service and later must bolt onto whatever they built on their terms; that leaves me worse off than if we had both stayed legacy, and for them it is the worst case because they bear the full migration risk without enjoying network-wide scale. If both banks stay on the old rails, operations remain stable and we share the same limitations, but we miss the cost and safety gains of the new system; that is a safe but clearly second-best outcome for each side.

So my decision is whether to commit our bank to migrate now or keep us on the legacy platform for this cycle, while the other bank independently decides at the same time whether to migrate now or stay legacy.
story_col:
I'm the CFO of the rival bank that, together with our competitor, handles most of the country's large-value payments; supervisors want us to modernize to a shared real-time settlement system but are leaving the timing up to each of us. A joint move could secure long-term efficiency and regulatory goodwill, but any botched migration by one bank would disrupt both of our clients and could spark a broader loss of confidence in the payment system.

If both banks migrate now to the shared real-time platform, we share costs, cut fraud and liquidity needs, and regulators see a coordinated, well-managed upgrade; that is plainly the best outcome for us and for them. If they migrate while we stay on the legacy rails, we avoid the immediate implementation risk but risk losing clients to their faster service and later having to integrate on their terms; for us that is worse than if both banks stayed legacy but still better than us migrating alone, and for them it is risky with only moderate upside because they lack our volume. If we migrate while they stay legacy, we carry almost all the project risk, any outage or compliance incident is blamed on us, and without their flows we do not get the scale benefits; that is clearly our worst case and also dangerous for them because any systemic disruption hits their customers too. If both banks stay legacy, our operations remain predictable and we share the same constraints, but we give up the gains of the new platform; that is a safe but clearly second-best result for each side compared with coordinated migration.

So we must choose whether to migrate now or stay on the legacy system this cycle, knowing they are simultaneously deciding whether to migrate now or stay legacy as well.
quality_justification: Realistic, detailed financial-infrastructure upgrade with clear operational and systemic risk stakes; roles mirror each other well, though safety focus is more implicit than explicit.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs encode stag-hunt-like incentives but each side’s ranking of miscoordination outcomes differs from the prose, and mutual legacy is relatively high, weakening the cooperative risk tension.
issues: ['Some contradictions between narrative and numeric ranking of off-diagonal outcomes.', 'Legacy–legacy payoff (7) close to joint migration (10), undercuts ‘clearly second-best’ description.', 'Not all statements about which outcome is worst for each player match the payoff matrix.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic banking-rail migration story with meaningful safety externalities; broadly stag-hunt-like, but payoff numbers only partially match the narrated preference orderings and tensions.

justification: User and assistant both choose how much to rely on/encourage trust. Payoffs depend on the other’s stance; mutual calibrated trust is best, miscalibration is risky. Coordination/Stag-hunt/No-conflict structures can model this.
story_row:
I head the analytics division at a tech firm, and my counterpart Dana runs the core platform group. A government agency has issued a huge RFP that only makes sense if both of our teams pool most of next year's budget to build a fully integrated solution. Alternatively, each of us can ignore the RFP and focus on renewing our smaller but very reliable existing contracts.

If we both shift our budgets to the joint bid, we will probably hit the technical requirements, have a serious shot at winning the mega deal, and both of our careers and teams would gain the most. If I move my whole roadmap onto the joint bid while Dana stays with the safe renewals, my team will miss our existing deals, almost certainly fail to deliver the integrated product alone, and I take the worst hit while Dana still books steady revenue. If I keep my team on the safe renewals while Dana goes after the big bid alone, my numbers stay acceptable but I look timid, lose influence if that bid works, and I am worse off than if we had both just stayed conservative, while Dana takes on a big solo risk. If we both stay with the smaller contracts, our revenue is stable and our jobs are secure, which is clearly better for both of us than one of us crashing on the big bid alone but still worse than jointly winning the mega contract.

Right now I must choose whether to reallocate my budget to chase the joint mega bid or to stick with the safe renewals, while Dana independently decides whether to do the same or to stay safe.
story_col:
I run the core platform group at our tech firm, and Alex leads the analytics division. A government agency has released a very large RFP that really requires both of our product lines to be tightly integrated, which would mean each of us diverting most of next year's budget. The alternative for both of us is to pass on that RFP and just focus on renewing our current, smaller contracts that are almost guaranteed.

If Alex and I both push our budgets into the integrated bid, we can build the full solution, have a strong chance of landing the huge contract, and that outcome gives both of us the biggest boost in revenue, headcount, and reputation. If I commit my group to the big bid while Alex stays with the safe renewals, I carry the integration work alone, likely miss the RFP requirements, and I end up in the worst position while Alex still delivers stable results. If I stay with the safe renewals while Alex chases the big bid alone, I keep predictable revenue and avoid disaster but I look overly cautious, lose strategic leverage if the bid somehow succeeds, and am less well off than if we had both just stayed with the conservative contracts, while Alex shoulders major risk. If we both stay with the smaller renewals, we maintain solid numbers and job security, which is clearly better for each of us than one person failing on the big bid while the other plays it safe, but not as valuable as succeeding together on the mega opportunity.

I now have to choose whether to move my budget into the joint high-stakes bid or to stay with the safe renewals, while Alex makes the same choice on the analytics side.
quality_justification: Realistic corporate strategy/career-risk scenario with clear incentives, but not explicitly tied to safety, regulation, or harm prevention; stakes are mostly commercial/political.
equilibria_justification: Clear stag hunt: (Go big,Go big) best for both; (Stay,Stay) safer second-best; unilateral Go big worst. Stories and payoffs align and are symmetric.
issues: ['Context is business risk/reward, not clearly a safety or harm-focused scenario.']
overall_comment: Well-written, symmetric stag-hunt story with coherent payoffs and strategic tension, but oriented around commercial upside vs caution rather than a true safety-related dilemma.
