story_row:
I lead the state highway interdiction team. Wiretap intel says a cartel will move a multi‑million‑dollar fentanyl shipment tonight using one of two parallel highways out of the city, A or B. We only have enough people and vehicles to fully cover one highway with a hard checkpoint; the other will be essentially unmonitored. If we guess right, we dismantle a major route and likely save lives; if we guess wrong, the load goes through and the cartel’s position strengthens.

If I send my entire unit to Highway A and they choose A, we set up a hard checkpoint, catch the truck, seize the cargo, and put the driver and organizers in prison—huge win for us, total disaster for them. If I cover A and they instead drive B, my officers sit there doing nothing while the shipment glides through on B—big failure for us, huge payday and safety for them. If I choose B and they risk A, we again miss everything while they profit and stay free. If I choose B and they also take B, we slam the door on them, seize everything, and they face long sentences—again our best outcome and their worst.

Right now I must decide whether to commit my full team to Patrol A or Patrol B, while the smuggling crew simultaneously decides whether to Drive A or Drive B.
story_col:
I coordinate transport for a cartel crew moving a single truck loaded with enough fentanyl to set us up for months. There are two realistic outbound highways tonight, A and B, and we know the state interdiction unit only has enough people to fully cover one with a serious checkpoint. If we slip through clean, we get paid, keep our status, and avoid prison; if we hit their line, we lose the load, the truck, and probably our freedom.

If they put their checkpoint on Highway A and I send the truck on A, we get stopped, the drugs are seized, our phones and ledgers are taken, and we’re facing long sentences—that’s our nightmare and their big victory. If they sit on A and I send the truck on B, we roll straight past, earn our full payout, and they look like they picked the wrong road—our best case and their worst. If they commit to B and I gamble on A, we again slide by untouched while they waste a night on the wrong stretch of road. If they wait on B and I still choose B, we drive straight into their trap, lose everything, and hand them a headline arrest.

So I have to decide whether to Drive A or Drive B with the shipment, while they independently choose whether to set up their checkpoint to Patrol A or Patrol B.
quality_justification: Highly realistic interdiction vs smuggling scenario, clear high stakes, concrete operational details, well-matched dual perspectives; tightly grounded in public safety.
equilibria_justification: Narrative describes pure coordination/anti-coordination of routes, but payoff matrix is matching-pennies (zero-sum, opposite preferences). Text suggests mutual best when matched, but game makes mismatch best for smuggler.
issues: ['Mismatch between story (both prefer same-road outcomes as decisive wins/losses) and formal matching-pennies payoffs where players have opposing preferences over matching vs mismatching.', 'Described incentives sound symmetric in intensity but not structured as a zero-sum game; qualitative stakes could allow some non-zero-sum framing.']
overall_comment: Strong, vivid safety contextualization, but incentives implied in the stories conflict with the matching-pennies payoff structure. Needs payoff/text adjustment to cleanly instantiate the intended game.

justification: Nonviolent crimes involve offenders vs. victims/defenders, each reacting to others’ choices. Crime vs. deterrence/defense can be modeled as PD, Chicken (risk of escalation), or Matching Pennies (attack vs. protect targets).
story_row:
I lead a joint customs task force at a coastal city. Intelligence says a cartel will try to move a massive fentanyl shipment ashore tonight, either through the north pier or the south pier, and we only have enough officers and scanners to lock down one pier properly; the other can only be lightly watched. If we guess wrong, that load reaches the streets, and my team will be held responsible.

If I commit the full team to the north pier and they send the freighter there, we hit them hard: we seize the drugs, make high-profile arrests, and likely cripple their operation; that’s the best possible night for us and a disaster for them. If I commit to the north but they divert to the south, the shipment moves through almost untouched, they cash in and expand, and I face career-ending scrutiny; that’s our worst outcome and their ideal. If instead I fully cover the south while they steer into the north, the same kind of failure happens in reverse: they win big while we get nothing. But if I fully cover the south and they choose the south, we again catch them cleanly, another top outcome for us and the worst one for them.

Right now I have to decide where to deploy my full-strength team—concentrate on the north pier or on the south pier—while the cartel’s logistics chief decides whether to route the freighter through the north approach or the south approach.
story_col:
I coordinate maritime logistics for a trafficking network moving a record fentanyl load tonight. Authorities know something is coming but not exactly where; they can only lock down one of the two cargo piers, north or south, with full inspections, leaving the other mostly open. If we pick the wrong approach and they’re waiting, this shipment is gone and several of us may end up in prison.

If they concentrate their officers and scanners on the north pier and we sail into the north approach, we lose everything: the drugs are seized, careers are ruined, and some of us may never see daylight again; that’s our absolute worst case and their biggest win. If they focus on the north and we instead land at the south pier, we slide through, sell the entire load, and greatly strengthen our position; that’s the best possible result for us and the one they most want to avoid. If they bet on the south while we come in at the north, we again enjoy that same huge success while they get nothing. But if they put all their resources on the south and we choose the south, we walk into a trap, which is once again disastrous for us and excellent for them.

I have to choose whether to route the freighter through the north pier or the south pier, at the same time their task force commander decides whether to lock down the north or the south.
quality_justification: Concrete customs-vs-cartel setting, clear stakes (massive fentanyl load, careers, prison), roles and options well specified, timing and constraints realistic for an interdiction scenario.
equilibria_justification: Story describes pure coordination/anti-coordination with aligned/opposed outcomes, but payoffs are identical on both diagonals and for off-diagonals, matching a coordination/zero-sum mix, not matching pennies.
issues: ['Game does not implement matching pennies: payoffs do not alternate win/lose across all four cells.', 'Narrative implies zero-sum interdiction vs smuggling, but payoff structure is symmetric across guarded/unguarded choices.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic interdiction scenario with clear opposing incentives, but the payoff matrix does not correspond to a matching pennies game; structure is closer to a simple zero-sum success/failure model than the intended template.

justification: Attacker vs target/defender: attacker trades gains from exposure vs risk of sanctions; defender invests in protection or not. Clear multi-actor incentives, can be PD, Chicken, or adversarial Matching Pennies.
story_row:
I’m the incident commander for city police during a credible terror threat. Intelligence says a small extremist cell will strike within the hour, but they can only hit one of two packed sites: the downtown mall or the central train station. I have one fully equipped SWAT team and can only lock down one location properly; the other will remain relatively exposed.

If I send SWAT to the mall and they attack the mall, we almost certainly intercept them, prevent mass casualties, and I’m praised for making the right call while their operation is ruined. If I send SWAT to the mall and they instead attack the station, the station is left soft, many people die, and I face public outrage and career-ending blame while they see the attack as a total success. If I send SWAT to the station and they hit the mall, the disaster is just as bad for us and just as good for them as in the previous case. If I send SWAT to the station and they attack the station, we stop them there with the same level of success and recognition as at the mall, and their group loses everything.

Right now I must choose whether to concentrate our best forces at the mall or at the station, while the attackers independently choose which single site to hit.
story_col:
I’m leading a small extremist cell ready to carry out one bombing today. Our explosives and people are enough for one major attack, and we’ve narrowed it down to two crowded targets: the downtown mall or the central train station. We know the police can fully secure only one of them with a heavy tactical presence before we move.

If they put their main team at the mall and we also go for the mall, we’ll likely be stopped, killed or captured, our message never gets out, and the operation becomes a complete failure, which is the worst outcome for us. If they stack their forces at the mall and we instead hit the station, security there will be weak, the bomb is likely to succeed, we get maximum impact, and they are seen as having failed. If they concentrate at the station while we choose the mall, we get the same level of success and impact at the mall as we would have had at the station, while they face the same blame. If they guard the station and we also attack the station, we again walk straight into their strongest point and the operation collapses just as badly as if we had picked the mall under heavy security.

We now have to decide whether to attack the mall or the station, while the police commander decides which single site to lock down with their main team.
quality_justification: Concrete, high‑stakes terror threat with clear roles, constraints, and consequences. Actionable and realistic, though somewhat stylized for pure symmetry.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs match opposing incentives: each prefers to mismatch the other, disliking matches. This fits anti-coordination but given symmetric preferences it’s closer to a zero-sum coordination/anti-coordination mix than classic matching pennies.
issues: ['Game is labeled Matching Pennies but described payoffs are symmetric anti-coordination with |8| payoffs, closer to zero-sum but not canonical matching pennies.']
overall_comment: Strong, realistic safety scenario with clear opposing incentives and payoffs. Minor mismatch with the strict matching pennies template but strategically coherent and interpretable as an anti-coordination/zero-sum game.

justification: Attacker vs defender: attacker chooses to inject or not; defender chooses strict or permissive filtering. Payoffs depend on each other’s choices, with conflict and exploit–defend dynamics (PD/Chicken/Matching Pennies).
story_row:
I am the chief information security officer for a major online bank. A credible intel feed shows a top-tier criminal group preparing a live attack in the next hour. We know from scans that two weak points exist: the customer database cluster and the public API gateway, and with the staff and maintenance window I have right now, I can only fully lock down one of them before the attack hits.

If I put my whole team on hardening the database and they choose to go after the database, we stop the intrusion cold, avoid mass data theft and catastrophic fines, and they waste their best exploit and weeks of preparation; that is the cleanest win for me and their most frustrating outcome. If I harden the database but they instead hit the gateway, they can still knock services offline and pivot into some front-end systems, causing major disruption and reputational damage for us while giving them a meaningful success, though losing the gateway would still be less destructive than having the database breached. If I shift all defenses to the gateway while they go straight for the unprotected database, we suffer a devastating breach of millions of accounts and harsh regulatory sanctions, while they score their biggest possible payday. If I cover the gateway and they also choose to attack the gateway, we probably absorb the attack with manageable downtime, they burn tools for little gain, and although the database remains a worrying weak spot this round, stopping a hit on the gateway is still my next-best outcome after blocking a direct shot at the database.

In the next few minutes I must choose whether to fortify the database or fortify the gateway, while the attackers independently choose whether to aim their main strike at the database or at the gateway.
story_col:
I lead an elite cybercrime crew targeting a major online bank. Our reconnaissance shows two tempting entry points: the customer database cluster and the public API gateway. We only get one serious shot tonight before our infrastructure and malware families are burned, and we know their security team can fully reinforce only one of those systems in time.

If their team locks down the database and I still direct our main exploit at the database, we slam into hardened defenses, burn our most valuable zero-day, and walk away empty-handed; that is the worst outcome for us and a huge relief for them. If they lock down the database but I instead push the attack through the weaker gateway, we can disrupt customer access, grab useful session data, and maybe pivot into some mid-tier systems, which is a solid win for us even if it does not touch the real crown jewels. If they focus their protection on the gateway while I drive the attack straight at the exposed database, we can quietly siphon millions of account records and transaction histories, our biggest possible payoff and their nightmare scenario. If they shore up the gateway and I also hit the gateway, we again waste time and tools on a hardened target and get almost nothing, but at least we have not burned our best database exploit.

Right now I must decide whether to point the operation at the database or at the gateway, while their security chief independently decides which one to heavily fortify.
quality_justification: Highly realistic cyber incident response scenario with clear roles, concrete stakes (data breach vs downtime), and time pressure; both perspectives are detailed and consistent.
equilibria_justification: Payoffs match narratives and strategic tensions well, but the matrix is a strict coordination/anti-coordination hybrid, not true matching pennies as labeled.
issues: ['Formal game is labeled Matching pennies but payoff structure is not zero-sum/anti-coordination; it resembles a dominance-structured security game instead.']
overall_comment: Strong, grounded cyber defense vs offense contextualization with consistent incentives; main flaw is misclassification as matching pennies rather than a security/coordination-style game.
